data.title: OceanFirst outlines mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth targets for 2026 as Flushing merger advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OceanFirst Financial Corp. (OCFC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Maher reported "earnings per share of $0.23 on a fully diluted GAAP basis and $0.41 on a core basis," and highlighted "a fifth consecutive quarter of net interest income growth, which increased by $5 million or 5% as compared to the prior quarter and up 14% as compared to the prior-year quarter." Maher emphasized the company's "organic growth momentum" as a direct result of investments made in the first half of 2025 and pointed to a "merger agreement with Flushing Financial Corporation and an investment agreement with Warburg Pincus" as key strategic developments, stating the Flushing acquisition "will directly support our organic growth initiatives in New York, positioning OceanFirst as a scaled competitor in the deepest banking markets in the country."
  • President Joseph Lebel commented that loan originations "totaled just north of $1 billion for the second consecutive quarter and resulted in record quarterly loan growth of $474 million." Lebel noted the C&I business "grew 42% for the year" and pointed to the Premier Bank team's deposit growth of $90 million or 37% from the previous quarter.
  • CFO Patrick Barrett stated, "Pretax pre-provision core earnings grew 9% or $3 million from the prior quarter, driven by earning asset growth over the second half of the year." Barrett added, "Average interest-earning assets increased meaningfully compared to the prior quarter," and confirmed "core noninterest expenses decreased from $72.4 million to $71.2 million, driven by the sale of our title business."

Outlook

  • Barrett stated, "There are no changes to our full year guidance, as stated in the third quarter's earnings release, mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth. NII and NIM growing with NIM growing past 3% during the year and NII ramping in the second half of the year. Other income, $7 million to $9 million per quarter and expenses relatively flat to current run rates." He clarified these are standalone expectations and "do not reflect the impact of the Flushing acquisition."
  • Barrett also provided a first quarter outlook: "first quarter core operating expense run rate to remain in the range of $70 million to $71 million, with seasonal compensation increases offset by a full quarter's benefit of our residential outsourcing initiatives."

Financial Results

  • Maher reported "net interest margin of 2.87% declined modestly compared to the third quarter." Total loans for the quarter "increased $474 million, representing an 18% annualized growth rate, driven by $1 billion in originations."
  • Asset quality remained strong with "total loans classified as special mention and substandard decreased 10% to $112 million or just 1% of total loans." Nonperforming loans to total loans were at 0.2% and nonperforming assets to total assets at 0.22%.
  • Core operating expenses of $71 million were "down $1 million or 2% from the linked quarter" due to the outsourcing of the residential lending platform. GAAP operating expenses were $84 million, including $13 million of noncore expenses.
  • Capital levels were described as "robust with an estimated Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio of 10.7% and tangible book value per share increased to $19.79."

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked for clarification on net interest income guidance and the relationship to loan growth. Barrett replied, "it actually will probably grow at a bit higher clip than whatever our loan balances grow just because of the compounding effect of how big the balance sheet is today." Tamayo also inquired about loan sales post-Flushing merger; Maher responded it is "too early to give you any precise figures on that" but expects to "do some work on the balance sheet in a way that improves our margins and ROA outlook over time while also reducing credit risk."
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: Asked about C&I growth pace for 2026. Lebel projected "similar growth rates...in the 7% to 9% range." Switzer also asked if the $2 billion to $3 billion deposit target by 2027 for Premier Bank is still valid. Lebel confirmed, "I don't see any reason why we would back off the 2027 targets."
  • Christopher Marinac, Janney Montgomery Scott: Queried about deposit rate trends and Flushing integration. Maher highlighted "a tremendous opportunity" for deposit growth and branch network advantages, while noting the potential for efficiency gains through balance sheet restructuring post-merger.
  • David Bishop, Hovde Group: Asked about geographic drivers of C&I growth and early-stage delinquency uptick. Lebel said growth is "pretty geographically dispersed" and Maher attributed the delinquency to "just one loan" with a federal government lease, not a long-term concern.
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens Inc.: Inquired about loan-to-deposit ratio outlook. Maher expressed a preference to see it "down under 100%" over time but not expecting "dramatically under 100%." Breese also asked about Flushing's deposit mix, to which Maher detailed the distinction between higher-cost and high-quality deposits.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarity around loan growth outlook, integration of the Flushing acquisition, and deposit cost trends, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought specifics on growth sustainability and merger benefits.
  • Management maintained a confident and slightly optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases such as "we're very pleased" and "I don't see any reason why we would back off the 2027 targets," while also noting "too early" to provide some merger-related specifics.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, the tone remained confident with a continued emphasis on disciplined growth and expense management, though Q4 included more forward-looking merger commentary.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Loan originations and growth accelerated compared to Q3, with Q4 reporting record quarterly loan growth of $474 million versus $373 million previously.
  • Deposit growth also increased, with Q4 total deposits up $528 million compared to $203 million in Q3. The Premier Bank team's deposit growth and customer acquisition continued at a strong pace.
  • Asset quality metrics improved further, with special mention and substandard loans declining both in dollar terms and as a percentage of total loans.
  • Operating expenses on a core basis fell, driven by the impact of the residential outsourcing initiative, in contrast to Q3 where restructuring charges were highlighted.
  • Management's tone remained confident, but commentary in Q4 placed more emphasis on the strategic impact and expected benefits of the Flushing merger. Analyst focus shifted toward the integration and future balance sheet optimization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management mentioned the need for further review of the Flushing loan and deposit portfolios post-close, indicating some uncertainty around balance sheet restructuring and ultimate margin impact.
  • Noninterest income decreased due to lower title fees and a reduction in gain on sale of loans linked to outsourcing, with management expecting measured runoff in the residential portfolio.
  • Asset quality concerns were addressed, with Maher explaining a specific uptick in early-stage delinquencies was tied to a single government-related loan, not viewed as a systemic risk.
  • Management cited seasonality and promotional deposit pricing as factors that could influence deposit costs and margin near-term.

Final Takeaway

OceanFirst Financial Corp. closed 2025 with strong loan and deposit growth, effective expense control, and robust asset quality. Management reaffirmed guidance for mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth in 2026 and highlighted the anticipated strategic benefits of the Flushing Financial merger, positioning the company for further expansion in key Northeast markets. The company remains focused on organic growth, operational efficiency initiatives, and is prepared to optimize the combined balance sheet as integration progresses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alaska Air expects 'meaningful' improvement in 2026 EPS
data.description:
Alaska Air Group (ALK) is trending higher and building on Friday’s premarket gains as the company’s remarks during its earnings call on Friday morning helped offset mixed fourth quarter results.

Reflecting better-than-expected capacity ((ASM)) and lower-than-expected costs per available seat mile, ex-fuel ((CASMex)), Alaska Air (ALK) earned an adjusted profit of $0.43 per share, down from $0.97 a year ago, but $0.32 better than Wall Street’s expectations and more than $0.30 better than what the company anticipated.

Revenue of $3.63B increased 3% from a year earlier and resulted in a 0.6% increase in year-over-year revenue per available seat mile ((RASM)) despite the government shutdown but was $10M short of the consensus estimates.   

Premium revenue increased 7%, cargo revenue was up 22%, and loyalty revenue increased 12% from the same quarter last year.

Looking ahead to the first quarter and fiscal year, Alaska Air (ALK) cautions that to hit the higher end of guidance, the macroeconomic recovery will need to be sustained in 2026 and for fuel prices to stabilize.

“Given the inherent uncertainty of the macroeconomic environment, we remain as focused as ever on controlling what is within our control, including disciplined cost management,” the company said in a statement.

During the earnings call Friday morning, CEO Ben Minicucci noted that while 2025 “did not deliver the financial returns we laid out at the start of the year, we strongly delivered against our Alaska Accelerate vision,” adding that 2026 bookings are “very encouraging.”

The three-year Alaska Accelerate initiative aims to generate $1B in incremental profit by expanding long-haul international flights, upgrading its premium offerings, launching a new premium credit card, and investing over $600M to support the combined cargo and fleet operations with a goal of at least 12 widebody international destinations by 2030.

To achieve this, Minicucci said that the company will focus on “harvesting the investments from 2025” and expects full-year earnings to be in the range of $3.50 to $6.50 per share, representing a “meaningful improvement” over 2025.  

Meanwhile, Chief Financial Officer Shane Tackett highlighted the growth in its high-margin premium cabin segment and improvement in its main cabin segment.

“We are excited to see continued growth in our premium cabin revenue and now have 86% of our 218 Boeing 737 seat retrofits complete,” Tackett said on the call, adding that Alaska Air (ALK) has also tapped into the lucrative international corporate travel space.

Accordingly, shares of Alaska Air Group (ALK) pierced resistance at the 200-day moving average with a gain of as much as 6% into the second half of Friday’s trading session.

data.title: Coherus Oncology a new outperform at Oppenheimer on tagmokitug potential
data.description:
• Oppenheimer has initiated Coherus Oncology (CHRS) at outperform, saying that its anti-CCR8 monoclonal antibody tagmokitug for various cancers has great promise.
  • The firm set a $10 price target (~529% upside based on Jan. 22 close).
  • Shares are up ~31% in Friday trading.
  • Analyst Jay Olson noted that while CCR8 is a relatively new target, "large-pharma investment in other anti-CCR8 programs provides external mechanistic validation as emerging clinical data supports confidence in class-wide safety and activity."
  • Tagmokitug is in phase 2 as a fourth-line treatment for colorectal cancer. It is in phase 1 for head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, as well as gastric and esophageal cancers.
  • Olson also called anti-IL-27 antibody casdozokitug "a high-potential, early-stage opportunity."
  • He added that Coherus' sole marketed treatment, Loqtorizi (toripalimab), a PD-1 inhibitor for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, could see growth as part of combination therapies.

data.title: Webster Financial targets 5%–7% loan growth and $3B revenue in 2026 while strengthening HSA Bank opportunities
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Webster Financial Corporation (WBS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Ciulla, Chairman, President & CEO, highlighted that "Webster continued to excel from a fundamental perspective in the fourth quarter, and we enter 2026 on our front foot." He reported a 17% ROATCE and a 1.2% ROAA for the full year, with EPS growth of 10%, loans up 8%, and deposits up 6%. Ciulla emphasized strategic investments in nontraditional banking verticals: "We produced strong financial results while continuing to invest in our nontraditional banking verticals including HSA Bank, Ametros and interSYNC, as we look to fortify and advance the strategic advantages these businesses provide."
  • Luis Massiani, President & COO, stated, "Limited payoff activity also contributed to better-than-expected loan growth in the fourth quarter," and cited significant progress on credit remediation: "Classified commercial loans were down 7% and nonperformers were down 8%. Net charge-offs were 35 basis points."
  • Massiani noted early-stage growth in HSA accounts tied to Bronze and Catastrophic plan participants and referenced the expectation of $1 billion to $2.5 billion in incremental deposit growth at HSA Bank over five years, including $50 million to $100 million in 2026.
  • William Holland, CFO & Executive VP, stated, "Balance sheet growth continued at a solid clip in the fourth quarter, with growth in both loans and deposits. Asset sets were up $880 million or 1% in the fourth quarter. On a full year basis, they were up just over $5 billion or 6.4%."
  • Holland reported, "Loan trends are highlighted... In total, loans were up $1.5 billion or 2.8%, and on a full year basis were up 7.8%." He added, "Deposit costs were down 11 basis points relative to the prior quarter."
  • Holland provided guidance: "We're anticipating loan growth of 5% to 7% and deposit growth of 4% to 6%. The midpoint of guide has expected revenue of $3 billion for 2026. On a GAAP basis, we expect net interest income of $2.57 billion to $2.63 billion."

Outlook

  • Holland outlined, "We expect fees to be $390 million to $410 million and expenses to be $1.46 billion to $1.48 billion." He also noted first quarter 2026 expenses will likely be a few percentage points higher than Q4, citing payroll taxes and annual merits as factors.
  • Management stated the expectation that HSA Bank's newly eligible participants will drive gradual, incremental deposit growth and that technology investments are complete to support this.
  • Guidance language reflects a continuation of moderate growth, with emphasis on risk mitigation and capital discipline.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted PPNR was down $4.9 million from the prior quarter, with slightly better revenue offset by higher expenses related to growth initiatives. Adjusted net income was slightly higher than the prior quarter due to lower provision and tax rate, and adjusted EPS benefited from a lower share count.
  • Tangible book value per share increased to $37.20 from $36.42 in the prior quarter.
  • Nonperforming assets were down 8% and commercial classified loans down 7%. Charge-offs for the quarter were 35 basis points.
  • Loan growth for Q4 was $1.5 billion or 2.8% and deposits were up 0.9% over the prior quarter.

Q&A

  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked about impact of the Marathon partnership on loan growth. Ciulla responded, "We're live, and we're operational... we have originated loans for the JV. And as I said, we've been more competitive... but we just haven't seen a real change in the dynamic in the sponsor book as of yet."
  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Asked about Category 4 threshold changes and NIM trajectory. Holland replied, "We're not anticipating the additional incremental $20 million of expense this year because we're able to either potentially avoid some of those expenses or certainly have more time to spread out those expenses into the future."
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: Asked about aggressive deposit pricing. Holland said, "We ended December with an average cost of deposits at 1.91% versus 1.99% for the quarter."
  • Jackson Singleton, Autonomous: Queried on loan growth guidance. Ciulla explained, "We may be de-emphasizing some businesses and really looking at kind of core franchise building, full relationships."
  • Christopher O'Connell, KBW: Asked about borrowing trends and fee guide. Holland attributed movements to seasonality and sub-debt redemption, noting fee guide variability is due to volatility in certain categories.
  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: Inquired about HSA open enrollment and buyback pace. Ciulla confirmed "opened up approximately about 15,000 more accounts than what we had at this point in '25" and expects continued share repurchases.
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about office loan book and noninterest-bearing deposits. Ciulla said, "We've materially reduced criticized, classified and nonaccrual loans... we don't see any significant deterioration in that portfolio."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about loan yields and leverage lending guidance. Ciulla clarified loan payoffs, not yields, were better than anticipated, and leverage lending guidance withdrawal provides some flexibility but doesn't alter financial outlook.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a generally neutral-to-positive tone, focusing on strategic growth opportunities, credit quality, and expense management. Their questions pressed for more color on loan growth, deposit pricing, and regulatory changes but did not indicate deep skepticism.
  • Management's tone was confident and measured during prepared remarks and remained consistent in Q&A responses, often expressing optimism—"We feel very good about the business and where it is today." When pressed on more uncertain areas, such as regulatory impacts, management was pragmatic and transparent—"I wish we could give more specific numbers."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment showed slightly greater interest in regulatory and strategic flexibility, while management's tone grew more assertive about capital strength and growth prospects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 was more explicit, projecting 5% to 7% loan growth and $3 billion revenue, compared to a less specific outlook previously.
  • Management's tone shifted from cautiously optimistic in Q3 to more confident in Q4, citing record achievements and strong forward positioning.
  • Analysts in Q4 focused more on execution of strategic initiatives (Marathon JV, HSA Bank growth) and their impact on results, while earlier questions centered more on credit quality and market risk.
  • Key metrics such as loan growth, deposit growth, and tangible book value per share showed continued improvement quarter-over-quarter.
  • Strategic focus evolved to emphasize technology readiness for HSA Bank and the potential for further capital return.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing competition in deposit pricing and noted the potential for seasonal volatility.
  • Credit risk in office and health care service portfolios persists but is described as "ring-fenced" and adequately reserved.
  • Regulatory changes, particularly around the Category 4 threshold, could influence expense trends and capital allocation.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the pace of noninterest-bearing deposit declines and the full realization of HSA Bank deposit growth.

Final Takeaway

Webster Financial Corporation closed 2025 with robust financial performance and strategic investments aimed at sustaining long-term growth. Management projects 5% to 7% loan growth and $3 billion in revenue for 2026, anchored by continued advancements in nontraditional banking verticals such as HSA Bank and new partnerships. The company maintains strong asset quality and capital levels, with ongoing attention to regulatory and market conditions shaping its forward outlook.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Janus Henderson acquires Richard Bernstein Advisors in SMA push
data.description:
Janus Henderson Group (JHG) has agreed to buy Richard Bernstein Advisors, an investment manager overseeing about $20B in client assets, it said on Friday, without disclosing financial terms.

The move will let Janus Henderson (JHG) significantly enhance its position in providing model portfolios and separately managed accounts, both of which are seeing accelerated client demand, noted Janus Henderson CEO Ali Dibadj.

RBA focuses on longer-term investment strategies that combine top-down, macroeconomic analysis and quantitatively-driven portfolio construction. Wall Street veteran Richard Bernstein, who founded RBA in 2009, will join Janus Henderson (JHG) as global head of macro and customized investing and enter into a multi-year deal with the company.

The transaction is expected to close in the second quarter.

JHG shares edged down 0.4% in afternoon trading.

data.title: Customers Bancorp targets 8–12% loan and deposit growth for 2026 as payments and AI drive expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Customers Bancorp (CUBI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jay Sidhu, Founder & Executive Chairman, opened by congratulating Samvir Sidhu on his CEO appointment and highlighted the company's transformation: "what began as an approximately $175 million troubled failing bank has now grown into a $25 billion asset institution recognized for its unique single point of contact strategy, its exceptional customer service focus and a forward-thinking approach to technology." He noted core EPS of $7.61, up from $5.60 in 2024, a 16% compounded annual growth rate in core deposits over six years, and industry-leading growth in revenue, earnings, and tangible book value.

  • Samvir Sidhu, President, CEO & Director, emphasized the company's Net Promoter Score increase to 81, nearly double the industry average. He outlined 2026 priorities: organic growth on both sides of the balance sheet, continued recruitment of top-performing teams, expansion of payments offerings, and leveraging AI for client experience and productivity. He stated, "2025 was any indication, top talent is excited about leveraging the unique platform here at Customers Bank." He highlighted the cubiX payments platform's $2 trillion payments volume in 2025, a 30% increase over the prior year, positioning the bank as "the #1 commercial payments network in the U.S. ahead of household names like Max and VISA."

  • Mark McCollom, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "Total deposits grew almost $400 million during the quarter, ending at just under $21 billion... up about $2 billion or 10% for the year." He noted a deposit beta of 54% for the quarter and 71% on interest-bearing deposits, with core franchise delivering $150 million noninterest-bearing growth in Q4. He also highlighted loan growth of $500 million quarter-over-quarter, net interest income of $204 million (22% year-over-year growth), and a net interest margin of 3.4%.

Outlook

  • Mark McCollom guided for 2026: loan growth of 8% to 12%, deposit growth net of remixing of 8% to 12%, and net interest income of $800 million to $830 million (7% to 11% growth). Noninterest expenses are projected at $440 million to $460 million (2% to 6% growth), with a targeted common equity Tier 1 ratio of 11.5% to 12.5% and an effective tax rate between 23% and 25%. He stated, "we are targeting loan growth of 8% to 12%. Led by the commercial teams we've onboarded and continue to recruit, we see deposit growth net of remixing of 8% to 12%."

Financial Results

  • Samvir Sidhu reported core EPS of $2.06 for the quarter, core ROE of 13.8%, and ROA of about 1.2%. Full-year core EPS reached $7.61, up 36% year-over-year. Deposits grew by $2 billion (10%), with $1.6 billion from new commercial banking teams. Loans grew 15% for the year, net interest income rose 15%, and the efficiency ratio dropped by over 6 percentage points. Tangible book value per share increased over 14% to $61.77.

  • Mark McCollom noted noninterest expense of $117 million for the quarter, impacted by $4.8 million in unique expenses, and an efficiency ratio of 49.5%. He also reported a tangible common equity ratio of 8.5%, up 90 basis points year-over-year, and net charge-offs declining 10% in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked about expectations for fee income and deposit growth drivers. Samvir Sidhu responded that average quarterly noninterest income has been about $30 million and highlighted the maturing of businesses that could drive further monetization. On deposit growth, he stated that guidance is driven mainly by the core commercial bank, with potential upside from payments and cubiX.

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Inquired about team recruitment and transaction volumes. Samvir Sidhu explained ongoing discussions with half a dozen teams and noted that $50 billion in projected payment volume relates to existing customers, aiming to deepen relationships and drive low-cost deposits. On loan growth, Mark McCollom said, "there's no one particular segment that we're more bullish about than the rest."

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked if expense guidance includes new team hiring. Mark McCollom confirmed it does and expects professional services fees to decline as unique costs subside.

  • Brian Wilczynski, Morgan Stanley: Queried about cubiX platform resilience amid crypto volatility. Samvir Sidhu explained that volatility increases network activity and that balances operate within a 10% threshold.

  • Peter Winter, D.A. Davidson: Asked about credit quality and margin outlook. Mark McCollom stated, "our credit quality was... starting from a very, very low base," attributing nonperforming increases to a single transaction. He guided that a margin of 3.25% is a good starting point for 2026.

  • Tyler Cacciatori, Stephens: Sought context on the cubiX customer base. Samvir Sidhu indicated the bank has hundreds of customers, with growth coming from both existing and new traditional finance nodes.

  • Harold Goetsch, B. Riley: Asked if instant payments cannibalize traditional rails. Samvir Sidhu said most new capabilities are incremental. On AI, he described initiatives in workflow orchestration, underwriting, and risk compliance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed optimism regarding growth and fee income potential but probed for details on deposit composition, expense control, and credit quality. Their tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focused on sustainability and drivers of growth.

  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing operational strength, recruitment momentum, and technological innovation. Samvir Sidhu frequently referenced ongoing initiatives and future prospects, stating, "we are seeing a big payoff from the investments we've made in our payments infrastructure."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained constructive, with analysts slightly more focused on granular drivers of deposit and loan growth and management more detailed in discussing competitive advantages and technology.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 includes explicit targets for 8–12% growth in both loans and deposits, compared to upwardly revised 2025 guidance in the prior quarter.

  • Strategic focus has broadened to emphasize payments leadership and AI deployment, compared to the prior quarter's emphasis on deposit transformation and team recruitment.

  • Analysts' questions were similar, maintaining focus on deposit quality, cubiX growth, and expense control, but with more in-depth queries about technology and competitive positioning.

  • Key metrics such as core EPS, ROE, and tangible book value per share continued their growth trends, while the efficiency ratio improved further.

  • Management's confidence level remains high, with more explicit discussion of competitive moats and future investments in technology.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the importance of maintaining strong credit quality and risk management while pursuing growth. Mark McCollom noted, "our credit quality was... starting from a very, very low base."

  • Analysts raised concerns about deposit remixing, exposure to digital asset volatility, and potential expense increases from team recruitment.

  • Samvir Sidhu addressed regulatory order status, stating the plan is "substantially done" and the focus for 2026 is to "put this behind us."

Final Takeaway

Customers Bancorp management emphasized the strength and durability of its core franchise, supported by robust deposit and loan growth, leading payments technology, and significant investment in AI. With clear targets for 2026, a strong pipeline of team recruitment, and a focus on operational excellence, the company remains confident in its ability to deliver sustainable shareholder value while maintaining prudent risk management and capital strength.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sibanye-Stillwater upgraded at HSBC, seeing extended PGM market deficit
data.description:
Sibanye-Stillwater (SBSW) +7.4% in Friday's trading to its highest in nearly four years as HSBC upgraded the miner to Buy from Hold with a $24.80/share price target for its ADRs, raised from $13.30, believing the company will benefit from rising prices for platinum group metals and gold.

PGM prices have increased sharply, and HSBC analyst Shilan Modi believes the rally reflects underlying bullish fundamentals, noting mine supply has been limited as previous low prices reduced investment and encouraged restructuring, thus sustained high prices may be needed ahead of an increase in mine supply.

The analyst said he expects the market deficit in the 3E PGMs - platinum, palladium, and rhodium - to extend into 2026, including a widening deficit for platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) over the next five years.

PGM miners have indicated they required higher prices to sustain or raise production volumes, and Modi said prices have moved above the required levels; the analyst said PGM miners typically prioritize deleveraging and increasing cash returns at this phase of the cycle, but he sees a possible increase in reinvestment capital in the next 12-24 months as the companies seek to stabilize volumes and potentially seek growth over the next decade.

data.title: Glacier Bancorp targets mid-50s efficiency ratio in 2026 amid acquisition-driven growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Glacier Bancorp, Inc. (GBCI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Randall Chesler, President and CEO, stated that 2025 was "a transformative year for Glacier Bancorp," highlighting the successful closures of the Bank of Idaho and Guaranty Bank & Trust acquisitions, which expanded the company's footprint into Idaho and Texas. Chesler emphasized, "These markets offer strong growth potential and fit seamlessly with our long-term growth strategy." The company ended the year with $32 billion in total assets, a record for Glacier Bancorp.
  • Chesler reported, "Net income was $63.8 million for the quarter, including the $36 million of expenses related to our 2025 acquisitions," and for the full year, "Net income for 2025 was $239 million, an increase of $48.9 million or 26% from the prior year."
  • Ron Copher, Executive VP, CFO & Secretary: "Q2 through Q4, I would estimate it will range, and this is for each of the 3 remaining quarters, $187 million to $192 million...the full year guide would be $750 million to, say, $766 million for the full year. Again, that's core operating expense."
  • Byron Pollan, Senior VP & Treasurer, added, "We've seen tremendous progress in our net interest margin. We've got great momentum, and we continue to see momentum ahead of us."

Outlook

  • Management is guiding for core noninterest expense in Q1 2026 of $189 million to $193 million, with expectations that expense will "step down" over Q2, Q3, and Q4.
  • The full-year 2026 core operating expense is projected in the range of $750 million to $766 million.
  • Efficiency ratio is expected to reach "mid-50s, 54% to 55%, which is our traditional range" by the second half of 2026, according to Pollan.
  • On loan growth, Tom Dolan, Senior VP & Chief Credit Officer, stated, "We're looking at low to mid-single digits for the full year," with a record pipeline at the start of 2026 and potential to be "towards the higher end of that range."

Financial Results

  • Total assets ended 2025 at $32 billion, with a loan portfolio of $21 billion at year-end, up $2 billion from the prior quarter and $3.7 billion for the year.
  • Total deposits reached $24.6 billion, increasing $2.7 billion from the prior quarter and $4 billion over the year.
  • Net interest margin for the quarter was 3.58%, an increase of 19 basis points from the prior quarter.
  • Diluted earnings per share for Q4 2025 was $0.49; for the full year, $1.99 per share, up $0.31 (18%) from the prior year.
  • Noninterest income for the quarter totaled $40 million, up $5 million over the prior quarter.
  • Noninterest expense for the quarter was $195 million, up $26.8 million primarily due to acquisition costs.
  • Tangible book value per share increased to $21, up 12% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Questioned the pace of organic loan growth and Guaranty’s contribution. Tom Dolan responded that seasonal slowdowns were expected but noted, "We are now at a record level of our pipeline early this year...a growing piece of the production is related to construction."
  • Feaster asked about margin trajectory and dependency on Fed action. Byron Pollan stated, "We have a lot of programmatic structural repricing drivers...we're not in any way Fed dependent."
  • Questions from Andrew Terrell, Stephens, and Kelly Motta, KBW, focused on expense guidance and loan yields. Copher confirmed, "Q2 through Q4, I would estimate...$187 million to $192 million," and Chesler noted higher new loan pricing margins.
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler, asked about deposit costs and cost savings from Guaranty. Pollan said, "the uptick that you saw was from the acquisition, and we do expect to see a declining deposit costs from here."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised pointed questions on loan growth, margin sustainability, cost guidance, and integration progress, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone with focus on clarity and confirmation of growth drivers.
  • Management’s tone remained confident throughout, with frequent reminders of progress and discipline. Chesler’s closing statement, "Very excited about the trends here and the growth into '26," and Pollan’s assertion, "We've seen tremendous progress in our net interest margin," reinforced this confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a steady, constructive tone with increased focus on integration outcomes and expense discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the completion of two strategic acquisitions, while the previous quarter only noted the completion of Bank of Idaho and the closing of Guaranty Bank & Trust.
  • Guidance for expense run rates became more specific, with management projecting $750 million to $766 million in core operating expenses for 2026, compared to more preliminary expense discussions last quarter.
  • Margin expansion continued, with the net interest margin increasing to 3.58% from 3.39% in Q3, and management reaffirmed the 4% target for the second half of 2026.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted more toward the integration of recent acquisitions, near-term efficiency gains, and expense control, while management expressed greater confidence in achieving traditional efficiency levels.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged seasonality in loan growth, early term payoffs in 2025, and integration costs associated with acquisitions.
  • Copher noted, "Q1 is typically our higher first quarter because we have the merit pay increases, employment taxes and then it will drop down."
  • Analysts queried about the sustainability of loan yield expansion and the realization of cost saves, with management indicating careful monitoring and discipline in both areas.

Final Takeaway

Glacier Bancorp closed 2025 with record asset growth, two significant acquisitions, and expanding geographic reach. The company detailed clear guidance for expense control, targeting a return to mid-50s efficiency ratios in 2026, and expects continued loan and margin growth supported by robust pipelines and repricing momentum. Management remains confident in achieving integration milestones and delivering further shareholder value in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apple plans to complete rollout of ad expansion in App Store search by late March
data.description:
Last year, Apple (AAPL) revealed it was going to allow for increased advertising by developers in its App Store during user searches in 2026.

The Cupertino-based company has now confirmed the advertising opportunity expansion will be available in all markets by the end of March.

"When a user searches on the App Store, your ad can appear at the top of their search results," Apple said. "And starting in March 2026, we’ll be introducing more ads to increase opportunity in search results."

About 65% of app downloads in the App Store occur directly after a search.

"To help give advertisers more opportunities to drive downloads from search results, Apple Ads will introduce additional ads across search queries," the company said.

Advertisers do not need to modify their campaign to be eligible for new positions. The ad format will be the same in any position, using a default product page or custom product page and an optional deep link. Advertisers will be billed as usual based on their pricing model, which is either cost per tap or cost per install. Advertisers can rely on default ads or create their own custom variation.

These ads are then matched to user searches based on relevant keywords. 

"If your app isn't relevant to what the user is searching for, it won't be displayed—regardless of how much you may be willing to pay," Apple said. "Apple Ads considers both relevance and bids and doesn't put apps into auctions if they're not a good match."

The App Store ecosystem generates hundreds of billions in revenue in developer billings and sales.

"Over the last five years, the size of the App Store ecosystem has nearly tripled from $142 billion in 2019 to $406 billion last year, and earnings for U.S.-based developers also more than doubled," Apple said, citing a study by the Boston University Questrom School of Business. "Small developers in particular have done exceptionally well as their earnings increased by 76 percent between 2021 and 2024."

data.title: Tractor Supply traffic soars ahead of massive winter storm
data.description:
Tractor Supply (TSCO) is seeing a surge of store and online traffic ahead of Winter Storm Fern, according to channel checks from Jefferies. The potentially historic winter storm is expected to blanket a large part of the U.S., including states in the South and Southwest not accustomed to snow and ice.

Analyst Jonathan Matuszewski pointed to a spike in daily web traffic to Tractor Supply's (TSCO) website from consumers in Texas, North Carolina, and Florida. He noted that Tractor Supply (TSCO) locations in East Texas have shelves cleared, while Oklahoma stores are out of propane tanks. "A store manager for the Tractor Supply in Lexington, North Carolina, indicated wood & fuel-burning stoves, kerosene, and generators are flying off the shelves for homes, and heat lamps for animals are seeing similar demand," updated Matuszewski. 

Matuszewski and his team believe that demand tied to the storm could boost Q1 top-line results.

Shares of Tractor Supply (TSCO) were up 1.8% in Friday afternoon trading. The retail stock is up about 8.7% on a year-to-date basis.
